1:
"Wherefore, holy brethren, partakers of the heavenly calling, consider the Apostle and High Priest of our profession, Christ Jesus;" The witnesses note the call to consider Christ as Apostle and High Priest. Gill notes the apostle calls the Hebrews "brethren," not because of natural lineage but because they were in the same spiritual relation, having the same Father, the adopted sons of God, the brethren of Christ. The witnesses note Paul exhorting the holy brethren, partakers of the heavenly calling, to consider the Apostle and High Priest of their profession, Christ Jesus; the summons to fix the mind on Christ in his twofold office — that Paul, drawing his conclusion ("wherefore") from the dignity of Christ already shown, addresses the believers as "holy brethren, partakers of the heavenly calling" (set apart, sharers in the call to heaven) and bids them "consider" (fix the attentive eye of faith upon) "the Apostle and High Priest of our profession, Christ Jesus" — naming him by two great offices: "Apostle" (the one sent from God to men, to declare his will, answering to and surpassing Moses) and "High Priest" (the one who goes from men to God, answering to and surpassing Aaron) — so that Christ unites in himself both offices, and is the great object of the believer's contemplation; consider the Apostle and High Priest of our profession, Christ Jesus. The witnesses note holy brethren, partakers of the heavenly calling. The witnesses note consider the Apostle and High Priest of our profession, Christ Jesus.

The witnesses note the call to consider Christ as Apostle and High Priest — the one sent from God to men, and the one who goes from men to God; both offices united in him.

So the witnesses make the verse the summons to consider Christ's twofold office. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer is to fix his mind on Christ, who unites in himself the offices of Apostle and High Priest. The witnesses note the holy brethren: "holy brethren, partakers of the heavenly calling" — Gill notes the spiritual relation, sons of God; the holy brethren; the the heavenly calling; the spiritual relation; the sons of God; the partakers. The expositors observe the consider: "consider" — fix the attentive eye of faith upon; the consider; the fix the eye; the of faith; the attentive; the contemplate. The witnesses press the Apostle: "the Apostle" — the one sent from God to men, surpassing Moses; the Apostle; the sent from God; the to men; the declare his will; the surpassing Moses. The expositors draw the lesson on the High Priest: "and High Priest of our profession" — the one who goes from men to God, surpassing Aaron; the High Priest; the from men to God; the surpassing Aaron; the the offering; the priest. The witnesses observe the both offices: Christ unites both offices in himself; the both offices; the united; the Apostle and Priest; the in himself; the one. The expositors note the object of contemplation: Christ is the great object of the believer's contemplation; the object of contemplation; the the believer; the fix the eye; the Christ; the consider. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the holy brethren partakers of the heavenly calling, the consider the Apostle and High Priest of our profession Christ Jesus, and the twofold office. The verse thus records the summons to consider Christ's twofold office — teaching that the believer is to fix his mind on Christ, who unites the offices of Apostle and High Priest.
2:
"Who was faithful to him that appointed him, as also Moses was faithful in all his house." The witnesses note Christ's faithfulness, compared with Moses. Gill notes "him that appointed him" or "made him" — Christ as man was made, but not as God; the apostle speaks not of his divine nature but of his offices, the constitution and settlement of him in office. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Christ was faithful to him that appointed him, as also Moses was faithful in all his house; the faithfulness common to Christ and Moses as the ground of comparison — that Paul, before showing Christ's superiority, first grants the point of likeness: Christ "was faithful to him that appointed him" (perfectly discharged the office God set him in, faithful in all his trust), "as also Moses was faithful in all his house" — for Moses too was a faithful servant, of whom God himself testified that he was faithful in all his house (Israel, the church of that age); so that the comparison begins on common ground, both being faithful in their charge, that the greater glory of Christ may then appear; faithful to him that appointed him, as also Moses was faithful. The witnesses note who was faithful to him that appointed him. The witnesses note as also Moses was faithful in all his house.

The witnesses note the faithfulness common to Christ and Moses — both faithful in their trust — the comparison beginning on common ground, that Christ's greater glory may then appear.

So the witnesses make the verse the shared faithfulness as the ground of comparison. The lesson the expositors draw is that both Christ and Moses were faithful in their charge, the likeness setting up the greater glory of Christ. The witnesses note the faithful: "who was faithful to him that appointed him" — Gill notes Christ faithful in the office God set him in; the faithful; the appointed him; the the office; the his trust; the discharged. The expositors observe the appointed: "him that appointed him" — God who constituted him in office, of his offices not his deity; the appointed; the constituted; the the office; the not his deity; the as man. The witnesses press the as Moses: "as also Moses was faithful" — Moses too was faithful; the as Moses; the also faithful; the the servant; the faithful; the trusted. The expositors draw the lesson on the all his house: "in all his house" — faithful in all God's house, Israel; the all his house; the Israel; the the church; the that age; the the house. The witnesses observe the common ground: the comparison begins on common ground; the common ground; the likeness; the both faithful; the the comparison; the begins. The expositors note the greater glory: that the greater glory of Christ may appear; the greater glory; the Christ; the appear; the superiority; the then. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the who was faithful to him that appointed him, the as also Moses was faithful in all his house, and the shared faithfulness. The verse thus records the shared faithfulness as the ground of comparison — teaching that both Christ and Moses were faithful in their charge, the likeness setting up the greater glory of Christ.
3:
"For this man was counted worthy of more glory than Moses, inasmuch as he who hath builded the house hath more honour than the house." The witnesses note Christ's superior glory as the builder over the house. Gill notes Moses was counted worthy of glory and honour, given him both by God and by men — by God in the work he called him to, to deliver Israel, reveal his will, and govern them. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Christ was counted worthy of more glory than Moses, as the builder of the house has more honour than the house; the superiority of Christ the builder over Moses who was but part of the house — that Paul, having granted Moses his glory, now shows Christ's to be greater, and proves it by a plain comparison: "he who hath builded the house hath more honour than the house" — Moses, for all his honour, was himself only a part of the house (the people of God), a member of it; but Christ is the builder and founder of that house, and as the architect is more honoured than the building he raises, so Christ, the builder, far surpasses Moses, who was but a stone in the building; for this man was counted worthy of more glory than Moses. The witnesses note this man was counted worthy of more glory than Moses. The witnesses note he who hath builded the house hath more honour than the house.

The witnesses note Christ's superior glory — as the builder of the house has more honour than the house; Moses but part of the house, Christ its builder and founder.

So the witnesses make the verse the superiority of Christ the builder. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, as the builder of God's house, far surpasses Moses, who was but part of that house. The witnesses note the more glory: "this man was counted worthy of more glory than Moses" — Christ has more glory than Moses; the more glory; the than Moses; the Christ; the surpasses; the greater. The expositors observe Moses' glory: Gill notes Moses had real glory from God and men; the Moses' glory; the from God; the from men; the real; the honour. The witnesses press the the builder: "he who hath builded the house" — Christ is the builder and founder; the the builder; the the founder; the the architect; the Christ; the raises. The expositors draw the lesson on the more honour: "hath more honour than the house" — the builder is more honoured than the building; the more honour; the the builder; the the building; the than the house; the surpasses. The witnesses observe the Moses part of the house: Moses was but a part of the house, a member; the Moses part; the part of the house; the a member; the a stone; the the building. The expositors note the Christ surpasses: so Christ, the builder, far surpasses Moses; the Christ surpasses; the the builder; the surpasses; the Moses; the greater. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the this man was counted worthy of more glory than Moses, the he who hath builded the house hath more honour than the house, and the superiority of the builder. The verse thus records the superiority of Christ the builder — teaching that Christ, as the builder of God's house, far surpasses Moses, who was but part of that house.
4:
"For every house is builded by some man; but he that built all things is God." The witnesses note that the universal builder is God, pointing to Christ's deity. Gill notes that every house is built by some one, for a house does not build itself — true of houses properly so called, and of nations, families, the church in general, particular congregations, and individual believers. The witnesses note Paul reasoning that every house is built by some man, but he that built all things is God; the builder of all is God — and Christ, the builder, is therefore God — that Paul presses his argument to its height with a great principle: "every house is builded by some man" (every house must have a builder), "but he that built all things is God" — the universal Builder, who made and frames all things, the whole creation and the whole church, is God himself; the witnesses note the force of this for Christ's deity — Christ has just been named the builder of God's house, and here the builder of all is declared to be God, so that the One who builds the house, Christ, is no creature like Moses but God, the maker of all; he that built all things is God. The witnesses note for every house is builded by some man. The witnesses note but he that built all things is God.

The witnesses note that the universal Builder is God — and since Christ is the builder of the house, the argument bears on his deity: the One who builds all is God, not a creature.

So the witnesses make the verse the builder of all being God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the universal Builder is God, and since Christ is the builder, the argument points to his deity. The witnesses note the every house: "every house is builded by some man" — Gill notes every house must have a builder; the every house; the a builder; the by some man; the not itself; the built. The expositors observe the all kinds: this is true of nations, families, the church, believers; the all kinds; the nations; the families; the the church; the believers. The witnesses press the built all things: "but he that built all things is God" — the universal Builder is God; the built all things; the the universal Builder; the all things; the the creation; the the church. The expositors draw the lesson on the God: "is God" — the maker of all is God himself; the God; the the maker; the all things; the himself; the the Builder. The witnesses observe the bears on Christ's deity: Christ being the builder of the house, the argument bears on his deity; the Christ's deity; the the builder; the the house; the no creature; the God. The expositors note the not a creature: Christ is no creature like Moses but God; the not a creature; the not like Moses; the but God; the the maker; the divine. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for every house is builded by some man, the but he that built all things is God, and the builder of all is God. The verse thus records the builder of all being God — teaching that the universal Builder is God, and since Christ is the builder, the argument points to his deity.
5:
"And Moses verily was faithful in all his house, as a servant, for a testimony of those things which were to be spoken after;" The witnesses note that Moses was faithful, but only as a servant. Gill notes Moses was not only a servant to the Israelites, but the Lord's servant — of his choosing, sending, and approving, serving in holy things heartily and sincerely. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Moses was faithful in all his house, but as a servant, for a testimony of the things to be spoken after; the honourable but subordinate place of Moses as a servant pointing to Christ — that Paul defines the precise nature of Moses' faithfulness, to set it below Christ's: Moses "verily was faithful in all his house" (truly and fully faithful, an honourable thing) — but "as a servant" (in the rank and place of a servant in the house, not its lord) — and even his ministry was prophetic, "for a testimony of those things which were to be spoken after," that is, all his service pointed forward, witnessing beforehand to the gospel and to Christ who was to come; so that Moses' very honour was that of a faithful servant testifying to another greater than himself; Moses verily was faithful in all his house, as a servant. The witnesses note Moses verily was faithful in all his house, as a servant. The witnesses note for a testimony of those things which were to be spoken after.

The witnesses note Moses faithful but as a servant — his very ministry prophetic, witnessing beforehand to the gospel and to Christ who was to come, greater than himself.

So the witnesses make the verse the subordinate place of Moses the servant. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses was faithful, but only as a servant whose ministry testified to Christ. The witnesses note the faithful: "Moses verily was faithful in all his house" — Gill notes truly faithful, the Lord's servant; the faithful; the all his house; the truly; the the Lord's servant; the honourable. The expositors observe the as a servant: "as a servant" — in the rank and place of a servant, not the lord; the as a servant; the the rank; the not the lord; the the place; the subordinate. The witnesses press the testimony: "for a testimony of those things which were to be spoken after" — his ministry was prophetic, witnessing beforehand; the testimony; the prophetic; the witnessing; the beforehand; the pointed. The expositors draw the lesson on the pointed to Christ: all his service pointed forward to the gospel and Christ; the pointed to Christ; the the gospel; the to come; the forward; the witnessing. The witnesses observe the another greater: Moses testified to another greater than himself; the another greater; the than himself; the Christ; the greater; the witnessing. The expositors note the honour of a servant: Moses' honour was that of a faithful servant; the honour of a servant; the faithful; the servant; the the honour; the subordinate. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Moses verily was faithful in all his house as a servant, the for a testimony of those things which were to be spoken after, and the servant testifying. The verse thus records the subordinate place of Moses the servant — teaching that Moses was faithful, but only as a servant whose ministry testified to Christ.
6:
"But Christ as a son over his own house; whose house are we, if we hold fast the confidence and the rejoicing of the hope firm unto the end." The witnesses note Christ as Son and Lord over his own house. Gill notes Christ is a Son, not by creation, adoption, or office, but by nature, the only begotten of the Father; and so Lord and master over his own house, which Moses was not. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Christ is as a Son over his own house, whose house we are, if we hold fast the confidence and rejoicing of hope firm to the end; the lordship of Christ the Son over the house and the believer's perseverance — that Paul crowns the comparison: while Moses served as a servant in the house, "Christ as a son over his own house" — not a servant but the Son and Lord, ruling over the house that is his own — and Paul applies it: "whose house are we" (believers being that very house of God in the gospel age), and adds the condition and evidence of belonging to it: "if we hold fast the confidence and the rejoicing of the hope firm unto the end"; the witnesses note this not as making salvation depend on us, but as marking that the true members of Christ's house are known by their persevering confidence and hope to the end; whose house are we, if we hold fast the confidence and the rejoicing of the hope firm unto the end. The witnesses note Christ as a son over his own house. The witnesses note whose house are we, if we hold fast the confidence and the rejoicing of the hope firm unto the end.

The witnesses note Christ as Son and Lord over his own house — and we are that house, if we hold fast our confidence and hope to the end; perseverance the mark of true members.

So the witnesses make the verse the lordship of Christ the Son and the believer's perseverance. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ is Son and Lord over his house, and the true members are marked by persevering confidence and hope. The witnesses note the a son: "but Christ as a son over his own house" — Gill notes Christ the Son by nature, Lord over his house; the a son; the by nature; the the Son; the Lord; the over his house. The expositors observe the his own house: "over his own house" — ruling the house that is his own; the his own house; the his own; the ruling; the Lord; the the house. The witnesses press the whose house are we: "whose house are we" — believers are that house of God; the whose house are we; the believers; the the house; the of God; the the gospel. The expositors draw the lesson on the hold fast: "if we hold fast the confidence and the rejoicing of the hope" — holding fast confidence and the joy of hope; the hold fast; the the confidence; the the rejoicing; the the hope; the persevere. The witnesses observe the firm to the end: "firm unto the end" — held firm to the end; the firm to the end; the to the end; the persevering; the firm; the endure. The expositors note the mark not condition: the perseverance marks the true members, not the ground of salvation; the mark not condition; the true members; the evidence. The verse thus records the lordship of Christ the Son and the believer's perseverance — teaching that Christ is Son and Lord over his house, and the true members are marked by persevering confidence and hope.
7:
"Wherefore (as the Holy Ghost saith, To day if ye will hear his voice," The witnesses note the solemn warning drawn from the Holy Ghost's word in the Psalm. Chrysostom notes that Paul, having treated of hope and said "we are his house if we hold fast," next shows that we ought to look forward with firmness, and proves this from the Scriptures, expressly ascribing the words to the Holy Ghost. The witnesses note Paul introducing the Spirit's word, "Today if ye will hear his voice," as the ground of a solemn warning; the divine voice of the Spirit calling for present obedience — that Paul, to enforce the perseverance just urged, turns to Scripture (Psalm 95), introducing the quotation with a weighty note of its authorship: "as the Holy Ghost saith" — the words are the very voice of the Spirit of God — and the word he cites is itself a present call: "To day if ye will hear his voice," a summons to hear and obey God's voice now, in the present "today" of opportunity; the witnesses note Paul's ascription of the Old Testament Scripture to the Holy Ghost as the divine Author, and the urgency of the "today" that presses immediate obedience; To day if ye will hear his voice. The witnesses note as the Holy Ghost saith. The witnesses note To day if ye will hear his voice.

The witnesses note the Spirit's word, "Today if ye will hear his voice" — the Scripture ascribed to the Holy Ghost as Author, the "today" pressing immediate obedience.

So the witnesses make the verse the Spirit's call for present obedience. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Scripture is the voice of the Holy Ghost, calling for obedience in the present "today." The witnesses note the Holy Ghost saith: "as the Holy Ghost saith" — Chrysostom notes Paul ascribes the words to the Holy Ghost; the Holy Ghost saith; the the Spirit; the the Author; the ascribed; the the voice. The expositors observe the from Scripture: Paul proves perseverance from the Scriptures; the from Scripture; the proves; the perseverance; the Psalm 95; the cited. The witnesses press the today: "To day" — the present time of opportunity; the today; the the present; the opportunity; the now; the the time. The expositors draw the lesson on the hear his voice: "if ye will hear his voice" — a summons to hear and obey God's voice; the hear his voice; the obey; the God's voice; the summons; the heed. The witnesses observe the divine Author: the Old Testament word is the voice of the Spirit, its divine Author; the divine Author; the the Spirit; the the voice; the Scripture; the inspired. The expositors note the urgency: the "today" presses immediate obedience; the urgency; the the today; the immediate; the obedience; the now. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the as the Holy Ghost saith, the To day if ye will hear his voice, and the call for present obedience. The verse thus records the Spirit's call for present obedience — teaching that the Scripture is the voice of the Holy Ghost, calling for obedience in the present "today."
8:
"Harden not your hearts, as in the provocation, in the day of temptation in the wilderness:" The witnesses note the warning against hardening the heart as Israel did. Gill notes there is a natural hardness of heart — the heart of man being like a stone, senseless, impenitent, and inflexible, on which no impressions can be made but by powerful grace — and an acquired, habitual hardness. The witnesses note Paul citing the warning, "Harden not your hearts, as in the provocation, in the day of temptation in the wilderness"; the call not to repeat Israel's hardening of heart against God — that Paul continues the Spirit's word from Psalm 95, applying Israel's history as a warning: "harden not your hearts" — do not, by unbelief and rebellion, stiffen and steel your hearts against God's voice — "as in the provocation, in the day of temptation in the wilderness," that is, as the fathers did in the wilderness, when at Meribah and Massah they provoked God and put him to the test by their murmuring; so that the present hearers are solemnly cautioned not to imitate that hardening which brought ruin on their fathers; harden not your hearts, as in the provocation. The witnesses note harden not your hearts. The witnesses note as in the provocation, in the day of temptation in the wilderness.

The witnesses note the warning not to harden the heart as Israel did in the wilderness — not to stiffen the heart against God's voice by unbelief, as the fathers did at Meribah.

So the witnesses make the verse the warning against hardening the heart. The lesson the expositors draw is that the hearers are not to repeat Israel's hardening of heart against God's voice. The witnesses note the harden not: "harden not your hearts" — Gill notes do not stiffen the heart by unbelief; the harden not; the the heart; the stiffen; the unbelief; the rebellion. The expositors observe the natural hardness: there is a natural hardness, the heart like a stone; the natural hardness; the like a stone; the senseless; the impenitent; the inflexible. The witnesses press the provocation: "as in the provocation" — as the fathers provoked God; the the provocation; the provoked; the the fathers; the rebellion; the murmuring. The expositors draw the lesson on the temptation: "in the day of temptation in the wilderness" — when they tested God at Meribah and Massah; the temptation; the tested; the the wilderness; the Meribah; the Massah. The witnesses observe the do not imitate: the hearers are not to imitate that hardening; the do not imitate; the the hardening; the the fathers; the warning; the caution. The expositors note the brought ruin: that hardening brought ruin on their fathers; the brought ruin; the the fathers; the ruin; the the warning; the perished. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the harden not your hearts, the as in the provocation in the day of temptation in the wilderness, and the warning against hardening. The verse thus records the warning against hardening the heart — teaching that the hearers are not to repeat Israel's hardening of heart against God's voice.
9:
"When your fathers tempted me, proved me, and saw my works forty years." The witnesses note Israel's tempting of God despite seeing his works. Gill notes the apostle cites this to expose the Jews' glorying in their ancestors, to dissuade them from following their sinful practices, and to deter them by observing both the sin and its punishment. The witnesses note Paul recalling that their fathers tempted God, proved him, and saw his works forty years; the aggravated sin of provoking God amid the sight of his wonders — that Paul recounts the fathers' guilt: "when your fathers tempted me, proved me" (put God to the trial, as if doubting his power and faithfulness, by their repeated unbelief and murmuring), and that with the deepest aggravation — "and saw my works forty years," for all the while they tempted him, they were beholding his mighty works of judgment and mercy, the daily manna, the cloud, the deliverances, for forty long years; so that their unbelief was the more inexcusable, sinning against the clearest and most prolonged evidence of God's presence and power; when your fathers tempted me, proved me, and saw my works forty years. The witnesses note when your fathers tempted me, proved me. The witnesses note and saw my works forty years.

The witnesses note Israel tempting God while seeing his works forty years — the aggravated sin of provoking God amid the clearest, most prolonged evidence of his presence.

So the witnesses make the verse the aggravated sin of tempting God amid his works. The lesson the expositors draw is that the fathers' unbelief was inexcusable, provoking God while beholding his works for forty years. The witnesses note the tempted: "when your fathers tempted me, proved me" — Gill notes they put God to the trial by unbelief; the tempted; the proved me; the the trial; the unbelief; the murmuring. The expositors observe the doubting: as if doubting his power and faithfulness; the doubting; the his power; the his faithfulness; the unbelief; the tested. The witnesses press the saw my works: "and saw my works" — they beheld his mighty works; the saw my works; the beheld; the his works; the the wonders; the the deliverances. The expositors draw the lesson on the forty years: "forty years" — for forty long years; the forty years; the long; the the manna; the the cloud; the prolonged. The witnesses observe the inexcusable: their unbelief was the more inexcusable; the inexcusable; the the more; the unbelief; the against evidence; the aggravated. The expositors note the clearest evidence: sinning against the clearest, most prolonged evidence; the clearest evidence; the prolonged; the God's presence; the his power; the against. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the when your fathers tempted me proved me, the and saw my works forty years, and the aggravated sin. The verse thus records the aggravated sin of tempting God amid his works — teaching that the fathers' unbelief was inexcusable, provoking God while beholding his works for forty years.
10:
"Wherefore I was grieved with that generation, and said, They do alway err in their heart; and they have not known my ways." The witnesses note God's grief at the erring generation. Gill notes that "the generation of the wilderness," as the Jews call them, was said to be more beloved than any generation, yet they will not allow them a part in the world to come; and God's being "grieved" is to be understood after the manner of men. The witnesses note Paul declaring that God was grieved with that generation, saying they always err in their heart and have not known his ways; the divine displeasure at a people incurably astray in heart — that Paul gives God's own verdict on the provoking generation: "I was grieved with that generation" (God was displeased and offended at them, the language of grief being used after the manner of men), and the ground of it: "they do alway err in their heart" (their wandering was not occasional but constant and inward, a habitual straying of the heart itself) "and they have not known my ways" (they remained ignorant of and indifferent to God's ways, refusing to learn them); so that the root of their ruin was a settled, heart-deep estrangement from God; they do alway err in their heart; and they have not known my ways. The witnesses note I was grieved with that generation. The witnesses note they do alway err in their heart; and they have not known my ways.

The witnesses note God's grief at the erring generation — always straying in heart, not knowing his ways; the root of their ruin a settled, heart-deep estrangement from God.

So the witnesses make the verse the divine displeasure at a people astray in heart. The lesson the expositors draw is that God was grieved at a generation incurably erring in heart and ignorant of his ways. The witnesses note the grieved: "I was grieved with that generation" — Gill notes God was displeased, the language of grief used humanly; the grieved; the displeased; the offended; the after men; the the generation. The expositors observe the alway err: "they do alway err in their heart" — their straying was constant and inward; the alway err; the constant; the inward; the the heart; the habitual. The witnesses press the in their heart: the wandering was of the heart itself; the in their heart; the the heart; the inward; the deep; the straying. The expositors draw the lesson on the not known my ways: "and they have not known my ways" — ignorant of and indifferent to God's ways; the not known my ways; the ignorant; the indifferent; the God's ways; the refusing. The witnesses observe the refusing to learn: they refused to learn God's ways; the refusing to learn; the refused; the God's ways; the indifferent; the ignorant. The expositors note the heart-deep estrangement: the root of ruin was a settled, heart-deep estrangement; the heart-deep estrangement; the settled; the the root; the ruin; the from God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I was grieved with that generation, the they do alway err in their heart and they have not known my ways, and the displeasure at the astray. The verse thus records the divine displeasure at a people astray in heart — teaching that God was grieved at a generation incurably erring in heart and ignorant of his ways.
11:
"So I sware in my wrath, They shall not enter into my rest.)" The witnesses note God's oath that the unbelievers should not enter his rest. Gill notes swearing is ascribed to God to show the certainty of the thing spoken of — as of mercies, when he swears in love; so here of punishment, when he swears in wrath, indignation, and sore displeasure, and the threatened evil is irrevocable and inevitable. The witnesses note Paul citing that God sware in his wrath that they should not enter into his rest; the irrevocable sentence of exclusion from God's rest — that Paul gives the dreadful issue of the fathers' unbelief: "so I sware in my wrath" — God, provoked at last, confirmed his sentence with an oath, the most solemn and unchangeable form of his word — "They shall not enter into my rest," that is, into the rest of the land of Canaan (and, beyond it, the rest of God); so that their unbelief shut them out by a sentence sealed with God's own oath, irreversible and certain, the carcasses of that generation falling in the wilderness, never to reach the promised rest; so I sware in my wrath, They shall not enter into my rest. The witnesses note so I sware in my wrath. The witnesses note They shall not enter into my rest.

The witnesses note God's oath in wrath that the unbelievers should not enter his rest — the irrevocable sentence of exclusion, sealed with God's own oath, shutting them out.

So the witnesses make the verse the irrevocable sentence of exclusion. The lesson the expositors draw is that God's oath in wrath shut the unbelievers out of his rest, irreversibly and certainly. The witnesses note the sware: "so I sware in my wrath" — Gill notes God confirmed the sentence with an oath; the sware; the the oath; the confirmed; the in wrath; the solemn. The expositors observe the certainty: swearing shows the certainty of the thing; the certainty; the the oath; the certain; the irrevocable; the sealed. The witnesses press the in wrath: "in my wrath" — in indignation and sore displeasure; the in wrath; the indignation; the displeasure; the provoked; the anger. The expositors draw the lesson on the not enter rest: "They shall not enter into my rest" — shut out of Canaan and God's rest; the not enter rest; the shut out; the Canaan; the God's rest; the excluded. The witnesses observe the irreversible: the sentence was irreversible and certain; the irreversible; the certain; the sealed; the the oath; the unchangeable. The expositors note the carcasses fell: the carcasses of that generation fell in the wilderness; the carcasses fell; the the generation; the the wilderness; the never reached; the perished. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the so I sware in my wrath, the They shall not enter into my rest, and the sentence of exclusion. The verse thus records the irrevocable sentence of exclusion — teaching that God's oath in wrath shut the unbelievers out of his rest, irreversibly and certainly.
12:
"Take heed, brethren, lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief, in departing from the living God." The witnesses note the warning to take heed against an unbelieving heart. Gill notes this exhortation is grounded on the case of their ancestors, as a warning; Paul styles them "brethren" to show he had no hard thoughts of them, and that his jealousy was a godly one, not an evil suspicion. The witnesses note Paul charging the brethren to take heed lest there be in any an evil heart of unbelief, departing from the living God; the solemn caution against the unbelief that leads to apostasy — that Paul presses the application of Israel's fall upon the present hearers: "take heed, brethren" (watch carefully over yourselves) "lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief" — for unbelief is no light fault but "an evil heart," a root of wickedness — and he names where it leads: "in departing from the living God," that is, the apostasy and falling away from the true and living God to which unbelief tends; so that each is to watch his own heart, knowing that an unbelieving heart, if unchecked, ends in departure from God; lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief. The witnesses note take heed, brethren, lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief. The witnesses note in departing from the living God.

The witnesses note the caution against an evil heart of unbelief — for unbelief is a root of wickedness that, unchecked, ends in departing from the living God.

So the witnesses make the verse the caution against the unbelief that leads to apostasy. The lesson the expositors draw is that each must watch his heart against the unbelief that ends in departure from God. The witnesses note the take heed: "take heed, brethren" — Gill notes watch carefully, a godly jealousy; the take heed; the watch; the godly jealousy; the brethren; the caution. The expositors observe the in any of you: "lest there be in any of you" — each is to watch his own heart; the in any of you; the each; the his own heart; the watch; the individual. The witnesses press the evil heart of unbelief: "an evil heart of unbelief" — unbelief a root of wickedness, an evil heart; the evil heart of unbelief; the a root; the wickedness; the no light fault; the evil. The expositors draw the lesson on the departing: "in departing from the living God" — the apostasy unbelief tends to; the departing; the apostasy; the falling away; the from God; the tends. The witnesses observe the unchecked: unbelief, if unchecked, ends in departure; the unchecked; the if unchecked; the ends; the departure; the apostasy. The expositors note the the living God: departure from the true and living God; the the living God; the the true God; the departure; the from God; the apostasy. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the take heed brethren lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief, the in departing from the living God, and the caution against unbelief. The verse thus records the caution against the unbelief that leads to apostasy — teaching that each must watch his heart against the unbelief that ends in departure from God.
13:
"But exhort one another daily, while it is called To day; lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin." The witnesses note the remedy — daily mutual exhortation against the hardening of sin. Gill notes the phrase is sometimes rendered "comfort one another," which the saints may do by discoursing together of divine things, by praying together, and by instructing one another in the doctrines of the gospel. The witnesses note Paul charging that they exhort one another daily, while it is called Today, lest any be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin; the daily mutual exhortation that guards against sin's hardening power — that Paul gives the preventive against the evil heart of unbelief: not solitary watchfulness only, but the mutual care of the body — "exhort one another daily" (admonish, encourage, and stir up one another every day, in constant brotherly care) "while it is called To day" (so long as the present season of opportunity lasts) — and he names the danger this guards against: "lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin," for sin is deceitful, hardening the heart by slow and unfelt degrees, and the daily exhortation of brethren is God's appointed means to keep any from being so deceived; but exhort one another daily, while it is called To day. The witnesses note but exhort one another daily, while it is called To day. The witnesses note lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin.

The witnesses note the remedy of daily mutual exhortation — guarding against sin's deceitful, slow hardening of the heart; brotherly care God's appointed means while it is "today."

So the witnesses make the verse the daily mutual exhortation against sin's hardening. The lesson the expositors draw is that the brethren are to exhort one another daily, lest sin deceitfully harden any heart. The witnesses note the exhort: "but exhort one another daily" — Gill notes admonish, encourage, instruct one another; the exhort; the one another; the daily; the admonish; the encourage. The expositors observe the mutual care: not solitary watchfulness but the mutual care of the body; the mutual care; the the body; the brotherly; the not solitary; the together. The witnesses press the while it is today: "while it is called To day" — so long as the present season lasts; the while it is today; the the present; the the season; the opportunity; the lasts. The expositors draw the lesson on the hardened: "lest any of you be hardened" — lest any be hardened; the hardened; the the heart; the stiffened; the any; the guarded. The witnesses observe the deceitfulness of sin: "through the deceitfulness of sin" — sin hardens by slow, unfelt degrees; the deceitfulness of sin; the deceitful; the slow; the unfelt; the hardening. The expositors note the appointed means: daily exhortation is God's appointed means; the appointed means; the daily exhortation; the God's means; the the brethren; the preventive. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the but exhort one another daily while it is called To day, the lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin, and the daily exhortation. The verse thus records the daily mutual exhortation against sin's hardening — teaching that the brethren are to exhort one another daily, lest sin deceitfully harden any heart.
14:
"For we are made partakers of Christ, if we hold the beginning of our confidence stedfast unto the end;" The witnesses note the partaking of Christ marked by steadfast perseverance. Gill notes "we are made partakers of Christ" — being loved by him, given to him, and chosen in him before the foundation of the world, and so participating of all spiritual blessings in him; the phrase expresses union to Christ. The witnesses note Paul declaring that we are made partakers of Christ, if we hold the beginning of our confidence steadfast to the end; the union with Christ evidenced by perseverance to the end — that Paul gives a further motive to the steadfastness urged: "we are made partakers of Christ" (sharers in him and all his benefits, united to him) — a great and blessed thing — and he adds the same evidence as before: "if we hold the beginning of our confidence stedfast unto the end"; the witnesses note this "if" not as the cause by which we become partakers, but as the proof and sign of it — those truly united to Christ are known by holding their first confidence firm to the end, persevering faith being the evidence of a real share in Christ; if we hold the beginning of our confidence stedfast unto the end. The witnesses note for we are made partakers of Christ. The witnesses note if we hold the beginning of our confidence stedfast unto the end.

The witnesses note the partaking of Christ evidenced by perseverance — those truly united to him hold their first confidence firm to the end; the "if" the proof, not the cause.

So the witnesses make the verse union with Christ evidenced by perseverance. The lesson the expositors draw is that the truly united to Christ are known by holding their first confidence firm to the end. The witnesses note the partakers: "we are made partakers of Christ" — Gill notes sharers in him and his benefits, united; the partakers; the sharers; the his benefits; the union; the in him. The expositors observe the chosen in him: chosen in him before the foundation of the world; the chosen in him; the before the foundation; the loved; the given; the union. The witnesses press the hold confidence: "if we hold the beginning of our confidence" — holding the first confidence; the hold confidence; the the beginning; the first; the faith; the firm. The expositors draw the lesson on the stedfast to the end: "stedfast unto the end" — held firm to the end; the stedfast to the end; the to the end; the firm; the persevering; the endure. The witnesses observe the proof not cause: the "if" is the proof and sign, not the cause; the proof not cause; the the sign; the not the cause; the evidence; the the mark. The expositors note the persevering faith: persevering faith is the evidence of a real share in Christ; the persevering faith; the the evidence; the a real share; the in Christ; the genuine. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for we are made partakers of Christ, the if we hold the beginning of our confidence stedfast unto the end, and the union evidenced by perseverance. The verse thus records union with Christ evidenced by perseverance — teaching that the truly united to Christ are known by holding their first confidence firm to the end.
15:
"While it is said, To day if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts, as in the provocation." The witnesses note the renewed pressing of the Spirit's word. Gill notes this repetition of the citation is in order to make a further improvement of it, which shows that the words belong to the present times of the gospel, and contain matter of present concern. The witnesses note Paul repeating, "Today if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts, as in the provocation"; the present urgency of the warning re-applied to the hearers — that Paul, before drawing his conclusions, repeats the Spirit's word once more to drive it home: "while it is said, To day if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts, as in the provocation" — the very fact that the Scripture still says "Today" shows that the warning is not a dead letter of the past but a living word of present concern, pressing the gospel hearers, now, while their "today" lasts, to hear God's voice and not harden their hearts as the fathers did; so that the call is renewed with all its urgency upon the present generation; To day if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts. The witnesses note while it is said, To day if ye will hear his voice. The witnesses note harden not your hearts, as in the provocation.

The witnesses note the renewed pressing of the Spirit's word — the very "Today" of Scripture showing the warning is no dead letter but a living word of present concern to the hearers.

So the witnesses make the verse the present urgency of the re-applied warning. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Scripture's "Today" presses the warning as a living word upon the present hearers. The witnesses note the while it is said: "while it is said" — Gill notes the repetition makes a further improvement; the while it is said; the repetition; the improvement; the further; the pressed. The expositors observe the present times: the words belong to the present times of the gospel; the present times; the the gospel; the present; the belong; the now. The witnesses press the today: "To day if ye will hear his voice" — the "today" of present opportunity; the today; the his voice; the opportunity; the now; the hear. The expositors draw the lesson on the not a dead letter: the warning is no dead letter but a living word; the not a dead letter; the living word; the present concern; the not the past; the living. The witnesses observe the re-applied: the call is re-applied to the present hearers; the re-applied; the the hearers; the the present; the renewed; the pressed. The expositors note the all urgency: renewed with all its urgency upon this generation; the all urgency; the renewed; the this generation; the urgent; the pressed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the while it is said To day if ye will hear his voice, the harden not your hearts as in the provocation, and the present urgency. The verse thus records the present urgency of the re-applied warning — teaching that the Scripture's "Today" presses the warning as a living word upon the present hearers.
16:
"For some, when they had heard, did provoke: howbeit not all that came out of Egypt by Moses." The witnesses note that those who provoked were the very ones who had heard. Gill notes "when they had heard" — the law on Mount Sinai, the voice of words, the book of the covenant, the whole system of laws delivered to Moses, and the gospel too, for that was preached to them in the wilderness. The witnesses note Paul observing that some who had heard did provoke, though not all who came out of Egypt by Moses; the provoking were those who heard, yet not without exception — that Paul, by a series of questions (vv.16-18), drives the lesson home. He first notes who it was that provoked God: "some, when they had heard, did provoke" — the very people who had heard God's voice and word were the ones who rebelled, so that hearing alone does not save — yet "howbeit not all that came out of Egypt by Moses," for there were exceptions (Caleb and Joshua, who believed); so that the mass of those who heard fell by provoking unbelief, though a faithful few were preserved; for some, when they had heard, did provoke. The witnesses note for some, when they had heard, did provoke. The witnesses note howbeit not all that came out of Egypt by Moses.

The witnesses note that the very ones who heard God's voice provoked him — hearing alone does not save — though not all, for a faithful few like Caleb and Joshua believed.

So the witnesses make the verse the provoking being those who heard. The lesson the expositors draw is that hearing alone does not save, for the very hearers provoked God, though a faithful few were preserved. The witnesses note the heard and provoked: "for some, when they had heard, did provoke" — Gill notes the hearers themselves rebelled; the heard and provoked; the the hearers; the rebelled; the God's voice; the provoked. The expositors observe the what they heard: they had heard the law and the gospel in the wilderness; the what they heard; the the law; the the gospel; the Sinai; the heard. The witnesses press the hearing not enough: hearing alone does not save; the hearing not enough; the not save; the the hearers; the provoked; the alone. The expositors draw the lesson on the not all: "howbeit not all that came out of Egypt" — not all, there were exceptions; the not all; the exceptions; the Caleb; the Joshua; the believed. The witnesses observe the faithful few: a faithful few believed and were preserved; the faithful few; the believed; the preserved; the Caleb and Joshua; the spared. The expositors note the mass fell: the mass of hearers fell by unbelief; the mass fell; the the mass; the unbelief; the provoking; the perished. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for some when they had heard did provoke, the howbeit not all that came out of Egypt by Moses, and the hearers who provoked. The verse thus records the provoking being those who heard — teaching that hearing alone does not save, for the very hearers provoked God, though a faithful few were preserved.
17:
"But with whom was he grieved forty years? was it not with them that had sinned, whose carcases fell in the wilderness?" The witnesses note the second question — God was grieved with the sinners whose bodies fell. Barnes notes "with whom was he grieved" — with whom was he angry? Was it not with them that had sinned, that had sinned in various ways, by rebellion, murmuring, and unbelief? The witnesses note Paul asking with whom God was grieved forty years — with them that had sinned, whose carcasses fell in the wilderness; the objects of God's grief were the sinners who perished in the desert — that Paul puts the second question and answers it: "with whom was he grieved forty years?" — and the answer is plain: "was it not with them that had sinned" — the rebels and murmurers and unbelievers — "whose carcases fell in the wilderness," that is, who died under God's judgment, their dead bodies strewn across the desert, never reaching the land; so that God's long displeasure fell upon the sinners, and the visible proof of it was that whole generation perishing in the wilderness without entering the rest; whose carcases fell in the wilderness. The witnesses note was it not with them that had sinned. The witnesses note whose carcases fell in the wilderness.

The witnesses note God's grief falling on the sinners — those who rebelled and disbelieved, whose carcasses fell in the wilderness; the visible proof of his long displeasure.

So the witnesses make the verse the objects of God's grief being the sinners who perished. The lesson the expositors draw is that God's long displeasure fell on the sinners, whose bodies perished in the wilderness. The witnesses note the grieved: "with whom was he grieved forty years?" — Barnes notes with whom was God angry; the grieved; the angry; the forty years; the the question; the displeasure. The expositors observe the them that sinned: "was it not with them that had sinned" — the rebels, murmurers, unbelievers; the them that sinned; the rebels; the murmurers; the unbelievers; the the sinners. The witnesses press the various ways: who sinned in various ways — rebellion, murmuring, unbelief; the various ways; the rebellion; the murmuring; the unbelief; the sinned. The expositors draw the lesson on the carcases fell: "whose carcases fell in the wilderness" — their dead bodies strewn across the desert; the carcases fell; the dead bodies; the the desert; the perished; the strewn. The witnesses observe the never reached: they never reached the land; the never reached; the the land; the the rest; the died; the perished. The expositors note the visible proof: the visible proof of God's displeasure; the visible proof; the displeasure; the the judgment; the perished; the the generation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the was it not with them that had sinned, the whose carcases fell in the wilderness, and the grief on the sinners. The verse thus records the objects of God's grief being the sinners who perished — teaching that God's long displeasure fell on the sinners, whose bodies perished in the wilderness.
18:
"And to whom sware he that they should not enter into his rest, but to them that believed not?" The witnesses note the third question — God's oath of exclusion fell on the unbelievers. Barnes notes "to them that believed not" — that did not confide in God; "Yet in this thing ye did not believe the Lord your God"; in consequence of this want of faith, God solemnly sware they should not enter. The witnesses note Paul asking to whom God sware they should not enter his rest — to them that believed not; the exclusion from rest was the penalty of unbelief — that Paul puts the third and climactic question, narrowing the cause of their fall to its root: "to whom sware he that they should not enter into his rest?" — and the answer fixes the precise sin: "but to them that believed not"; so that, while they had sinned in many ways, the one sin that shut them out of the rest, the sin against which God's oath was sworn, was unbelief — their refusal to trust God's word and promise — so that unbelief is singled out as the deadly, excluding sin, the very thing the Hebrews are warned to shun; but to them that believed not. The witnesses note to whom sware he that they should not enter into his rest. The witnesses note but to them that believed not.

The witnesses note the exclusion from rest as the penalty of unbelief — the one sin that shut them out, against which God's oath was sworn, was their refusal to trust his word.

So the witnesses make the verse the exclusion from rest as the penalty of unbelief. The lesson the expositors draw is that unbelief was the deadly, excluding sin against which God's oath was sworn. The witnesses note the sware: "to whom sware he that they should not enter into his rest?" — the third question on the oath of exclusion; the sware; the the oath; the not enter rest; the the question; the exclusion. The expositors observe the believed not: "but to them that believed not" — Barnes notes those who did not confide in God; the believed not; the not confide; the unbelief; the refusal; the the sin. The witnesses press the root sin: unbelief is narrowed as the root of their fall; the root sin; the the root; the their fall; the narrowed; the unbelief. The expositors draw the lesson on the one excluding sin: the one sin that shut them out was unbelief; the one excluding sin; the shut them out; the unbelief; the the rest; the the penalty. The witnesses observe the refusal to trust: their refusal to trust God's word and promise; the refusal to trust; the God's word; the the promise; the unbelief; the distrust. The expositors note the deadly sin: unbelief is singled out as the deadly, excluding sin; the deadly sin; the singled out; the excluding; the unbelief; the to shun. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the to whom sware he that they should not enter into his rest, the but to them that believed not, and the exclusion by unbelief. The verse thus records the exclusion from rest as the penalty of unbelief — teaching that unbelief was the deadly, excluding sin against which God's oath was sworn.
19:
"So we see that they could not enter in because of unbelief." The witnesses note the conclusion — unbelief barred them from the rest. Gill notes they could not enter into God's rest, the land of Canaan, for they died by the plague before the Lord and their carcasses fell in the wilderness before they came to it, and the reason was their unbelief. The witnesses note Paul concluding that they could not enter in because of unbelief; the plain lesson — unbelief is what shuts men out of God's rest — that Paul gathers the whole argument into one solemn conclusion: "so we see that they could not enter in because of unbelief" — the testimony of the Old Testament itself shows plainly that it was unbelief, and nothing else, that barred that generation from the promised rest, their carcasses falling in the wilderness; the witnesses note this as the great practical point of the whole passage — that unbelief is the fatal, excluding sin, and the warning to the Hebrews (and to all) is to beware of that same unbelief lest they too come short of God's rest; so we see that they could not enter in because of unbelief. The witnesses note so we see that they could not enter in. The witnesses note because of unbelief.

The witnesses note the conclusion — they could not enter because of unbelief; the plain lesson that unbelief is the fatal, excluding sin that bars men from God's rest.

So the witnesses make the verse the conclusion that unbelief bars from rest. The lesson the expositors draw is that unbelief, and nothing else, shut that generation out of God's rest, a warning to all. The witnesses note the could not enter: "so we see that they could not enter in" — Gill notes they could not enter Canaan, God's rest; the could not enter; the Canaan; the God's rest; the barred; the perished. The expositors observe the the testimony: the Old Testament testimony shows it plainly; the the testimony; the the Old Testament; the plainly; the shows; the proof. The witnesses press the because of unbelief: "because of unbelief" — it was unbelief, and nothing else; the because of unbelief; the unbelief; the nothing else; the the cause; the the reason. The expositors draw the lesson on the carcasses: their carcasses fell in the wilderness; the carcasses; the fell; the the wilderness; the perished; the died. The witnesses observe the the great point: this is the great practical point of the passage; the the great point; the practical; the the passage; the the lesson; the chief. The expositors note the beware: the warning to beware of that same unbelief; the beware; the the warning; the unbelief; the lest come short; the all. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the so we see that they could not enter in, the because of unbelief, and the unbelief barring from rest. The verse thus records the conclusion that unbelief bars from rest — teaching that unbelief, and nothing else, shut that generation out of God's rest, a warning to all.
